title: Acute Pancreatitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Internal Medicine, Gastroenterology
objectives: Apply diagnostic criteria for acute pancreatitis
Manage with aggressive fluid resuscitation and early nutrition

vignette.text:
A 48-year-old woman presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back for 12 hours. She reports nausea, vomiting, and the pain is worse when lying flat. She had a fatty meal last night.

Past Medical History: Gallstones diagnosed 1 year ago (declined surgery), hyperlipidemia

Medications: Atorvastatin 20mg daily

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.abdomen: Epigastric tenderness with guarding, decreased bowel sounds, no rebound

questions.stem: This patient has epigastric pain and lipase 1,842 U/L.
questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for acute pancreatitis?

options.text: ACUTE PANCREATITIS DIAGNOSIS (Revised Atlanta): Requires 2 of 3 criteria: (1) ABDOMINAL PAIN: Characteristic - epigastric, radiating to back; Acute onset, severe, persistent; Worse when supine, better leaning forward; (2) LIPASE OR AMYLASE ≥3x upper limit normal: Lipase preferred (more specific, stays elevated longer); Degree of elevation doesn't correlate with severity; (3) IMAGING FINDINGS: CT/MRI/US showing pancreatitis; Usually not needed if pain + enzymes present; Obtain if: Diagnosis uncertain, No improvement in 48-72h, Suspected complications; This patient: Characteristic pain + Lipase >3x ULN = MEETS CRITERIA (imaging not required for diagnosis)
CT scan required in all cases

questions.explanation: Acute Pancreatitis Diagnosis (Revised Atlanta): Need 2 of 3 criteria: (1) Characteristic pain (epigastric, radiating to back, acute, severe), (2) Lipase or amylase ≥3x ULN, (3) Imaging findings. LIPASE preferred over amylase. Imaging NOT required if pain + enzymes present. This patient meets criteria with pain + elevated lipase.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tenner S, et al. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline: Management of Acute Pancreatitis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2013;108(9):1400-1415. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to determine the etiology.
questions.question: What are the common causes of acute pancreatitis?

options.text: Drugs are the most common cause
Alcohol is the most common cause

ACUTE PANCREATITIS ETIOLOGIES (I GET SMASHED): GALLSTONES: 40% - most common; ALT >150 U/L highly suggestive (95% PPV); RUQ US for all patients; ALCOHOL: 30% - second most common; Usually requires years of heavy use; HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA: TG >1000 mg/dL; ERCP: Post-procedure (5-10% of ERCPs); DRUGS: Azathioprine, valproic acid, didanosine, etc.; TRAUMA; AUTOIMMUNE; SCORPION stings (rare); HYPERCALCEMIA; INFECTIOUS (mumps, CMV); IDIOPATHIC: 15-25%; Consider occult microlithiasis, SOD; This patient: Elevated ALT (224) + known gallstones = GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS

questions.explanation: Pancreatitis Etiologies (I GET SMASHED): GALLSTONES (40% - most common, ALT >150 highly suggestive), ALCOHOL (30%), HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA (TG >1000), ERCP, DRUGS, TRAUMA, AUTOIMMUNE, SCORPION, HYPERCALCEMIA, INFECTIONS. 15-25% idiopathic. This patient: Elevated ALT + known gallstones = GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS.

questions.question: How is pancreatitis severity classified?

options.text: PANCREATITIS SEVERITY (Revised Atlanta): MILD: No organ failure; No local/systemic complications; Most common (80%); Resolves in 1 week; MODERATELY SEVERE: Transient organ failure (<48h); AND/OR local complications (fluid collections); SEVERE: Persistent organ failure (>48h); Highest mortality (30-50%); ORGAN FAILURE DEFINITION (Modified Marshall): Cardiovascular: SBP <90 despite fluids; Respiratory: PaO2/FiO2 <300; Renal: Cr >1.9 (or >2x baseline); BEDSIDE SCORING: BISAP (0-5 points): BUN >25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age >60, Pleural effusion; ≥3 = higher mortality; Assess within 24 hours; This patient: Tachycardia + borderline hypotension + elevated Cr = concerning for developing organ failure

questions.explanation: Pancreatitis Severity (Revised Atlanta): MILD - No organ failure (80% of cases). MODERATELY SEVERE - Transient organ failure (<48h) or local complications. SEVERE - Persistent organ failure (>48h), 30-50% mortality. ORGAN FAILURE: CV (SBP <90), Respiratory (PaO2/FiO2 <300), Renal (Cr >1.9). BISAP score ≥3 = higher mortality. This patient has concerning signs for developing organ failure.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: How should IV fluids be managed in acute pancreatitis?

options.text: FLUID RESUSCITATION IN PANCREATITIS: GOAL-DIRECTED THERAPY: Target UOP 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr; Decrease BUN and hematocrit; Normalize heart rate; FLUID CHOICE: Lactated Ringer's PREFERRED; May reduce inflammation vs NS; NS acceptable alternative; RATE: Initial bolus: 15-20 mL/kg; Then 3 mL/kg/hr (moderate-severe); Titrate based on response; CAUTION - Avoid OVERRESUSCITATION: Abdominal compartment syndrome; Pulmonary edema; Associated with worse outcomes; MONITORING: Assess response q6-12h initially; BUN should decrease; Hematocrit should decrease (if elevated); Urine output should improve; Clinical reassessment critical; This patient: Elevated Hct (48%), elevated BUN, tachycardia = needs aggressive fluids initially

questions.explanation: Fluid Resuscitation: GOAL-DIRECTED targeting UOP 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr, decreasing BUN and Hct. LACTATED RINGER'S preferred. RATE: Bolus 15-20 mL/kg, then 3 mL/kg/hr, titrate to response. AVOID OVERRESUSCITATION (abdominal compartment syndrome, pulmonary edema). This patient with elevated Hct, BUN, and tachycardia needs aggressive initial fluids.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You're considering when to feed the patient.
questions.question: When and how should nutrition be provided?

options.text: NUTRITION IN PANCREATITIS: PARADIGM SHIFT - Early feeding is beneficial!; MILD PANCREATITIS: Start oral diet when: Nausea/vomiting resolved; Hunger returns; Pain improving; Can start within 24 hours; Low-fat solid diet OK (no need for clear liquids first); MODERATE-SEVERE: Enteral nutrition preferred over TPN; Start within 72 hours; Nasogastric OR nasojejunal (equally effective); TPN only if: Can't tolerate enteral; Ileus preventing enteral; BENEFITS OF EARLY ENTERAL: Maintains gut barrier; Reduces infection; Reduces mortality in severe cases; Shorter hospital stay; AVOID: Prolonged NPO (harmful); This patient: Once pain improving and no vomiting → start low-fat diet

questions.explanation: Nutrition in Pancreatitis: EARLY FEEDING is beneficial. MILD: Start oral diet within 24 hours when symptoms improving - low-fat solids OK (no clear liquid progression needed). MODERATE-SEVERE: Enteral nutrition within 72 hours, NG or NJ (equally effective), TPN only if enteral fails. Prolonged NPO is harmful. Early enteral maintains gut barrier, reduces infections.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is NUTRITION IN PANCREATITIS: PARADIGM SHIFT - Early feeding is beneficial!; MILD PANCREATITIS: Start oral diet when: Nausea/vomiting resolved; Hunger returns; Pain improving; Can start within 24 hours; Low-fat solid diet OK (no need for clear liquids first); MODERATE-SEVERE: Enteral nutrition preferred over TPN; Start within 72 hours; Nasogastric OR nasojejunal (equally effective); TPN only if: Can't tolerate enteral; Ileus preventing enteral; BENEFITS OF EARLY ENTERAL: Maintains gut barrier; Reduces infection; Reduces mortality in severe cases; Shorter hospital stay; AVOID: Prolonged NPO (harmful); This patient: Once pain improving and no vomiting → start low-fat diet because Nutrition in Pancreatitis: EARLY FEEDING is beneficial. MILD: Start oral diet within 24 hours when symptoms improving -

questions.question: How should gallstone pancreatitis be managed?

options.text: GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS MANAGEMENT: URGENT ERCP INDICATIONS: Cholangitis (fever + jaundice + pain); Persistent biliary obstruction (rising bilirubin); Should be done within 24-48 hours; NOT for uncomplicated gallstone pancreatitis; CHOLECYSTECTOMY TIMING: MILD: Same admission (within 7 days); Reduces recurrence from 25% to 2%; MODERATE-SEVERE: After resolution; Usually 4-6 weeks after discharge; If necrosis - may wait longer; NON-SURGICAL CANDIDATES: ERCP with sphincterotomy; Reduces recurrence but less effective than CCY; MRCP/EUS: If common bile duct status unclear; Before ERCP to confirm stone; This patient: No cholangitis → No urgent ERCP; Plan CCY this admission

questions.explanation: Gallstone Pancreatitis Management: URGENT ERCP only for cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction (not routine). CHOLECYSTECTOMY TIMING: Mild - same admission (reduces recurrence from 25% to 2%). Moderate-severe - after resolution (4-6 weeks). This patient: No cholangitis, plan CCY this admission.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the major complications of acute pancreatitis?

options.text: PANCREATITIS COMPLICATIONS: EARLY (<4 weeks): Acute peripancreatic fluid collection; No defined wall; May resolve spontaneously; Acute necrotic collection; Contains necrotic debris; LATE (>4 weeks): Pseudocyst: Encapsulated fluid; No necrosis; Walled-off necrosis (WON): Encapsulated necrosis; May become infected; INFECTED NECROSIS: Suspect if: Fever, leukocytosis, failure to improve; Gas in necrosis on CT; FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION for culture; Treatment: Antibiotics + drainage; 'Step-up' approach preferred: Antibiotics → Percutaneous drainage → Minimally invasive debridement → Surgery; OTHER: Splenic vein thrombosis; Pseudoaneurysm (bleeding risk); Pancreatic duct disruption; Diabetes mellitus (late)

questions.explanation: Pancreatitis Complications: EARLY (<4 weeks) - Acute fluid collections, acute necrotic collections. LATE (>4 weeks) - Pseudocyst (encapsulated fluid), Walled-off necrosis. INFECTED NECROSIS: Suspect with fever/failure to improve/gas on CT. 'Step-up' approach: Antibiotics → drainage → minimally invasive → surgery. Prophylactic antibiotics do NOT prevent infected necrosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Pancreatitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: 2 of 3 criteria met - characteristic pain + lipase >3x ULN; (2) DETERMINE ETIOLOGY: Elevated ALT + known gallstones = GALLSTONE PANCREATITIS; (3) ASSESS SEVERITY: Tachycardia, borderline hypotension, elevated BUN/Cr = at risk; Calculate BISAP; (4) AGGRESSIVE IV FLUIDS: LR bolus 15-20 mL/kg then 3 mL/kg/hr; Goal UOP 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr; Monitor response, avoid overresuscitation; (5) ANALGESIA: Opioids are safe; (6) NO URGENT ERCP: No cholangitis present; (7) EARLY NUTRITION: Start low-fat diet once improving; (8) CHOLECYSTECTOMY: Same admission for mild; (9) MONITOR FOR COMPLICATIONS: Repeat imaging only if not improving; (10) If develops infected necrosis: Step-up approach
NPO for 1 week then advance slowly

questions.explanation: Complete Approach: CONFIRM diagnosis (2 of 3 criteria). DETERMINE etiology (gallstones here). ASSESS severity (concerning signs present). AGGRESSIVE FLUIDS (LR, goal-directed). ANALGESIA (opioids safe). NO URGENT ERCP (no cholangitis). EARLY NUTRITION when improving. SAME-ADMISSION CHOLECYSTECTOMY for mild. MONITOR for complications, repeat imaging only if not improving.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected acute pancreatitis?

options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in acute pancreatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in acute pancreatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in acute pancreatitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of acute pancreatitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

B.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

mcqs.stem: When managing acute pancreatitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

B.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
